# A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet {#sec-dr-cr .appendix}

> *Reference appendix — to be drafted Summer 2026.*

## The five account types

| Type      | Normal balance | Debit increases? | Credit increases? |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Asset     | Debit          | Yes              | No                |
| Liability | Credit         | No               | Yes               |
| Equity    | Credit         | No               | Yes               |
| Revenue   | Credit         | No               | Yes               |
| Expense   | Debit          | Yes              | No                |

The mnemonic is **DEAL**: **D**ebits increase **E**xpenses and **A**ssets; **L**iabilities, equity, and revenue (the rest) increase with credits.

## The accounting equation behind it

Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Revenue and expenses are sub-accounts of equity (specifically, retained earnings). When revenue increases (credit) or expense increases (debit), retained earnings — and thus equity — moves accordingly.

## Why we use it at all

The double-entry system is a verification device. Every transaction has equal debits and credits; if the books do not balance, something is wrong. This is itself an information-economics solution: a recordkeeping system designed to make tampering and arithmetic error visible. (See Chapter 1 for the broader context.)

## Common transaction types

*To be drafted: a one-page table of canonical transactions and their journal entries.*

## When in doubt

Ask: did the asset/liability/equity *increase* or *decrease* as a result of this transaction? Then apply the table above.
